usage: Tool registry — stag beetle as precision machinery, exoskeleton as capability boundary
concept: Stag beetle viewed from above, body opened like a technical cutaway to reveal clockwork tool mechanisms. Mandibles grip different tools. Exoskeleton plates become permission gates. Legs become API endpoint connectors.
prompt: A remarkable 18th century natural history engraving of a large European stag beetle (Lucanus cervus) viewed from directly above in a specimen-plate layout, rendered in extremely fine black ink cross-hatching on pure white background, 16:9 landscape format. The beetle's body is drawn as a technical cutaway: the left half shows the biological exterior in full engraving detail — glossy elytra with dense parallel hatching, segmented legs with fine hair detail, the magnificent forked mandibles rendered with anatomical precision. The right half of the body opens like a hinged panel to reveal an intricate interior that dissolves into a clockwork mechanism: tiny gears labeled RECALL, REMEMBER, READ, WRITE mesh together in a precise arrangement. A central cam shaft connects all gears to the mandibles, which grip two different miniature tools — a quill pen and a magnifying lens. The beetle's six legs on the technical side dissolve into standardized connector pins, each labeled with a port-like designation. The exoskeleton plates where they meet the clockwork boundary have tiny lock symbols — these are the capability gates that control which internal mechanisms an agent can access. Around the beetle, arranged in a naturalist's specimen layout, are six smaller tool icons in circles: a memory chip, a file folder, a pen nib, a terminal cursor, a search lens, and a running gear — the six built-in tools, each drawn in the same engraving style. Fine parallel hatching throughout with stippled shadows. The dissolution from biology to machinery is seamless — same line weight, same hand-engraved quality. Hooke's Micrographia inspiration. Pure black ink on absolute white background. No color. No sepia. No cream. Stark monochrome. Scientific illustration precision meets engineering schematic.
